headlineSummary: Microsoft Copilot drew the clearest negative reaction, while Anthropic and OpenAI were both mixed-neutral with limited evidence and a close tie between them [E2][E1][E3].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot was the most negative entry, driven by a comment describing abrupt token-limit changes and arbitrary pricing [E2].
Anthropic showed mixed-neutral sentiment with limited evidence, as a comment on Claude Code criticized it for writing too much code and creating duplicate logic [E1].
OpenAI also showed mixed-neutral sentiment with limited evidence, with a comment calling its claims vague and less trustworthy [E3].

evidence.excerpt: I’ve been using Claude Code with Opus 4.7; it’s not that the code it produces is wrong, it simply tends to write too much of it. In my opinion it’s still worth thinking about a particular feature and finding the best way to fit it into your code because Claude will often just pick a layer of the stack (maybe presentation), and jam it in there. A couple weeks later you need this data somewhere else and Claude can’t reuse the code (maybe in the service layer) so it kind of “ports” it over. Unless a person is paying attention we now have the double the amount of code and duplicate logic. I don’t see AI tools like Claude getting better at this anytime soon.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly discusses Claude Code/Opus 4.7 and criticizes it for bloated, poorly reusable code and little expected improvement.

evidence.excerpt: One month I could use Github Copilot fully with no disruptions. The next month, after pricing changes, I’ve run out of tokens in two days. Such drastic changes tell me that pricing of tokens is arbitrary, and AI business is running out of money fast.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names GitHub Copilot and strongly criticizes sudden token-limit changes as arbitrary and financially unsound.

evidence.excerpt: But at least, unlike OpenAI vague claims I can share the output with you to see!

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly refers to OpenAI and criticizes its claims as vague, indicating distrust or skepticism.
